mnemonics.title: HARD for Hardwired
mnemonics.content: HARD = Hardware-Implemented, Adaptive (not), Rapid, Dedicated. Hardwired control uses hardware circuits, cannot adapt easily, is fast, and has dedicated wiring for each instruction.

mnemonics.title: MICRO for Microprogrammed
mnemonics.content: MICRO = Memory Inside Control ROm provides Ordered instructions. Microprogrammed control stores microinstructions in ROM/CW memory, and executes them in sequence to generate control signals.

mnemonics.title: RISC vs CISC Reminder
mnemonics.content: RISC = Reduced Instruction Set Computer uses HARDWIRED (simple, fast). CISC = Complex Instruction Set Computer uses MICROPROGRAMMED (flexible, many instructions).

mnemonics.title: Speed Comparison Phrase
mnemonics.content: Hardwired is 'HAPPY' (High-speed, Adaptable, Plain, Performance-oriented, Yes to RISC). Microprogrammed is 'SLOW' (Stored-program, Lots of microsteps, Extra fetch cycle, Writable control store).

mnemonics.title: Control Signal Generation
mnemonics.content: In HARDWIRED: Hardware Directly Generates Signals (HDGS). In MICROPROGRAMMED: Microcode Interprets Instructions then Generates Signals (MIGS).

acronyms.usage: The component that manages execution of instructions; can be hardwired or microprogrammed.
Used in hardwired control to define states and transitions for instruction execution.
Stores microprogram in microprogrammed control; also holds fixed control logic in some hardwired designs.
Hardware component used to implement combinational logic in hardwired control units.
Holds the current instruction being decoded by the control unit.
Writable storage (RAM) in microprogrammed control where microcode can be modified.
Used to select appropriate control signals based on instruction type and execution stage.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a massive physical switchboard with hundreds of hardwired connections. Each instruction has its own dedicated wire path from input to output. When an instruction arrives, the appropriate switches automatically connect—like a railway track system where each train (instruction) has a pre-laid unique path. No flexibility, but incredibly fast because electricity flows through dedicated copper wires.

visualTips.concept: Microprogrammed Control as an Instruction Manual
visualTips.visualization: Picture a thick instruction manual (microcode) sitting next to a robot (control unit). For each complex task (instruction), the robot flips through the manual, reads step-by-step micro-instructions, and performs actions accordingly. The manual can be rewritten (modifiable), but reading takes time (extra fetch cycle). This is like following a recipe versus knowing how to cook instinctively.

Imagine a traffic signal system. In HARDWIRED: signals change instantly based on hardware timers (synchronous). In MICROPROGRAMMED: each green/yellow/red light requires checking a sequence chart (microinstruction fetch), causing slight delays.

Draw a flowchart where HARDWIRED = a complex maze with fixed walls (cannot change), while MICROPROGRAMMED = a modular building with movable walls (can redesign rooms). Each room is a microinstruction step.

analogies.analogy: Like a custom-built dedicated factory line for one product. Once built, it cannot make other products, but it works extremely fast with zero setup time. Changing the product requires rebuilding the entire factory.

Like a general-purpose robotic arm with a programmable controller. It can be reprogrammed to make different products by loading new software (microcode). Slower than dedicated machinery, but flexible and easier to modify.
Hardwired = Engraving instructions in stone (permanent, difficult to change). Microprogrammed = Writing instructions on a whiteboard (erasable, easy to update).
Hardwired = Pressing a direct dial tone (instant connection). Microprogrammed = Calling a customer service that routes you through multiple menu options (slower but handles more queries).
Hardwired = Adding new dedicated highway lanes for each new vehicle type (expensive). Microprogrammed = Updating GPS software to handle new routes (cheap).
